title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an artist called Maya talking about a neighbourhood mural project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an artist called Maya talking about a neighbourhood mural project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: An artist called Maya describes a neighbourhood mural project.
extracts.audio_script: Good afternoon. I’m Maya, and I lead Colour Street, a project helping young people design and paint public murals in our neighbourhood. The project began because one large wall had become covered with old posters and marks. It is not the side of a school or sports hall, as many people assume. Our first mural site is the outside wall of a former [9]furniture factory[/9], now used by several small businesses. Its owners want the building to feel more welcoming and have given us permission to transform a long section facing the road.||Young people aged fourteen to nineteen can join without having studied art. During the first meetings, we look at local places and talk about what the community wants to see represented. Residents completed a survey and chose [10]local wildlife[/10] as the main theme, ahead of famous buildings or portraits. Before painting begins, everyone attends a practical session about [11]paint safety[/11], covering protective clothing, careful use of materials and how to keep the pavement clear for passers-by. The work will be supervised by professional artists at all times.||Beginners may imagine they will immediately paint large pictures on a wall. First, each person creates small ideas and colour plans in a [12]design notebook[/12], which we supply at the welcome session. Once the final picture has been agreed, young artists help prepare the wall by applying a [13]base coat[/13], giving the later colours a clean surface on which to appear clearly. It is less creative than adding the final images, perhaps, but it is essential for a mural that needs to survive wind and rain. We provide overalls and brushes; participants should wear old trainers.||The project is not only about a single wall. We will document the process so other neighbourhood groups can learn from it. A student team will record short [14]video diaries[/14], interviewing artists and residents at different stages. These will be shared online only after permission has been checked. We also offer additional creative events. Last year our popular extra activity was poster printing. This year places disappeared first for a [15]street photography[/15] workshop, in which students will capture colours, signs and patterns around the area without photographing people closely.||Everybody who attends at least six sessions will receive a set of [16]art materials[/16] donated by a local shop, encouraging them to continue creating work at home. When the first mural is complete, there will be an outdoor [17]opening celebration[/17] with music and refreshments, where residents can meet the young artists. Finally, we hope to involve more walls in future, but professional outdoor paint is expensive. Sales of postcards showing the finished design will help finance a second [18]community mural[/18] beside the local health centre. Registration closes next month, and the project timetable is available on our website.

dialogue.speaker: maya
dialogue.text: Good afternoon. I’m Maya, and I lead Colour Street, a project helping young people design and paint public murals in our neighbourhood. The project began because one large wall had become covered with old posters and marks. It is not the side of a school or sports hall, as many people assume. Our first mural site is the outside wall of a former furniture factory, now used by several small businesses. Its owners want the building to feel more welcoming and have given us permission to transform a long section facing the road.||Young people aged fourteen to nineteen can join without having studied art. During the first meetings, we look at local places and talk about what the community wants to see represented. Residents completed a survey and chose local wildlife as the main theme, ahead of famous buildings or portraits. Before painting begins, everyone attends a practical session about paint safety, covering protective clothing, careful use of materials and how to keep the pavement clear for passers-by. The work will be supervised by professional artists at all times.||Beginners may imagine they will immediately paint large pictures on a wall. First, each person creates small ideas and colour plans in a design notebook, which we supply at the welcome session. Once the final picture has been agreed, young artists help prepare the wall by applying a base coat, giving the later colours a clean surface on which to appear clearly. It is less creative than adding the final images, perhaps, but it is essential for a mural that needs to survive wind and rain. We provide overalls and brushes; participants should wear old trainers.||The project is not only about a single wall. We will document the process so other neighbourhood groups can learn from it. A student team will record short video diaries, interviewing artists and residents at different stages. These will be shared online only after permission has been checked. We also offer additional creative events. Last year our popular extra activity was poster printing. This year places disappeared first for a street photography workshop, in which students will capture colours, signs and patterns around the area without photographing people closely.||Everybody who attends at least six sessions will receive a set of art materials donated by a local shop, encouraging them to continue creating work at home. When the first mural is complete, there will be an outdoor opening celebration with music and refreshments, where residents can meet the young artists. Finally, we hope to involve more walls in future, but professional outdoor paint is expensive. Sales of postcards showing the finished design will help finance a second community mural beside the local health centre. Registration closes next month, and the project timetable is available on our website.
